name: Cinnamon

book_claims.claim: Has potential anti-diabetic effects: may lower blood glucose and reverse fructose-induced insulin resistance
book_claims.mechanism: Hydroxycinnamaldehyde has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Improves metabolic syndrome symptoms in rodents and humans.

book_claims.claim: Mild blood cholesterol-lowering effect in animals and humans
book_claims.mechanism: Not fully specified; likely related to hydroxycinnamaldehyde and other extracts

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis of 10 RCTs found cinnamon supplementation (120 mg - 6g/day) significantly reduced fasting blood glucose (mean -24.6 mg/dL), total cholesterol, LDL, and triglycerides, while increasing HDL. Consistent with the book's anti-diabetic and cholesterol claims.

Systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 RCTs confirmed cinnamon supplementation reduces fasting glucose and HbA1c in type 2 diabetes patients. Effect sizes were clinically meaningful (HbA1c reduction of ~0.8%).

Review identified multiple mechanisms for cinnamon's anti-diabetic effects: mimics insulin signalling, enhances glucose uptake, improves glycogen synthesis, and inhibits gluconeogenesis. Ceylon cinnamon preferred over cassia due to lower coumarin content.

Moderate evidence for blood glucose and HbA1c reduction. Most effective in diabetic populations. Important distinction between Ceylon (Cinnamomum verum) and Cassia cinnamon -- cassia contains coumarin which can be hepatotoxic at high doses.

culinary_pairings.source: Classic combination in teas, desserts, and Middle Eastern dishes
Combined in golden milk and many curry blends
Warming spice pairing in chai, baking, and stir-fries
Essential in Moroccan tagines and Middle Eastern lentil soups
Classic breakfast combination; both lower blood glucose
Traditional in Mexican hot chocolate and European baking

seasonality: Available year-round as sticks or ground powder. Sticks retain flavour for up to 3 years. Ground cinnamon loses fragrance quickly -- buy fresh or grind from sticks. Ceylon cinnamon preferred for regular consumption due to lower coumarin levels.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Responsible for most anti-diabetic and antimicrobial activity. Well-absorbed orally.
Insulin-mimetic polyphenols that enhance glucose uptake. Type A proanthocyanidins are more bioavailable than Type B (found in most other foods).
Potential hepatotoxin at high doses. EU tolerable daily intake is 0.1 mg/kg body weight. Ceylon cinnamon contains 250x less coumarin than cassia -- strongly prefer Ceylon for daily use.
Very rich source, though typical serving sizes are small.

synergies.description: Both have anti-inflammatory and blood glucose-lowering properties; combined in golden milk
Both independently lower blood glucose; combined effect may be additive
